final_message:
All five functions are implemented in `problems.py`, stdlib-only (`re`, `collections.Counter`, `collections.deque`), with signatures and docstrings untouched:

- `parse_duration` — a single `re.fullmatch(r"(?:(\d+)h)?(?:(\d+)m)?(?:(\d+)s)?", s)` enforces ordering, duplicates, unknown units, whitespace, and trailing garbage in one shot; non-strings, empty matches, and any non-match raise `ValueError`. Multi-digit and over-range values work naturally.
- `luhn_valid` — strips spaces/hyphens, returns `False` (never raises) on empty or non-digit input, then runs the standard right-to-left doubling checksum. `"0"` passes, other single digits don't, as required.
- `flatten` — recursive walk joining keys with `sep`; empty dicts emit nothing, non-dict values (lists included) are leaf values, input is never mutated.
- `top_k_frequent` — `Counter` plus `sorted` on `(-count, word)`; `k <= 0` returns `[]`, oversized `k` just slices all distinct words.
- `rolling_max` — monotonic deque of indices; `ValueError` for `window <= 0`, `[]` for `window > len(nums)`.

Verified with a throwaway script covering every docstring example plus edge cases (ordering errors, duplicates, `"0"` Luhn, list leaves, tie-breaks, negative windows, etc.) and 500 randomized cross-checks of `rolling_max` against brute force. Performance: 1M elements with window 1000 in 0.39s, so the O(n) requirement has plenty of headroom.
